title: Cat and Kitten Rectal Prolapse: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your kitten has a rectal prolapse, it is a veterinary emergency. Learn the signs, the urgency, and why immediate care is necessary.
content_html:
What is Rectal Prolapse in Cats?

Rectal prolapse occurs when the internal lining of the rectum protrudes through the anal opening, appearing as a pink or red, tube-like mass. In kittens, this is often caused by severe straining due to diarrhea, intestinal parasites, or constipation. It is a distressing sight, but understanding the condition is the first step toward helping your pet.

Urgency Level: High Emergency

A rectal prolapse is considered a high-priority veterinary emergency. When the tissue remains outside the body, it loses its blood supply, leading to tissue death (necrosis) and potentially life-threatening infections. You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately. Do not attempt to push the tissue back in yourself, as this can cause permanent damage or internal tearing.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

If it is safe to do so, taking a clear photo of the prolapse before you head to the clinic can be incredibly helpful for the veterinary team. A photo allows the triage nurse to assess the size, color, and condition of the tissue immediately upon your arrival. It also helps the vet determine if the tissue is drying out or if there is active bleeding, ensuring your kitten receives the correct level of priority in a busy emergency room.

Immediate First Aid

While you are on your way to the vet, try to keep your kitten from licking or biting at the area. You can apply a small amount of water-based lubricant or a cool, damp cloth to the exposed tissue to keep it moist, but do not apply any pressure.

faqs.question: Can I treat a kitten's rectal prolapse at home?
faqs.answer: No. Rectal prolapse requires professional veterinary intervention to identify the underlying cause and safely reposition or surgically repair the tissue.

faqs.question: What causes rectal prolapse in kittens?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include heavy loads of intestinal parasites (worms), severe diarrhea, or chronic constipation, all of which cause the kitten to strain excessively.

faqs.question: Is rectal prolapse painful for cats?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is uncomfortable and can become very painful as the exposed tissue becomes inflamed, dry, or begins to lose blood circulation.
